note: Lehi's prophetic calling parallels Jeremiah's commission as a prophet to the nations.
Lehi's throne-room vision parallels Isaiah's vision of the Lord on His throne.
Nephi's faith to go and do echoes Abraham's faith to obey without knowing the destination.
The principle that one man should die to save a nation parallels Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy about Christ.
Lehi discovers his descent from Joseph who was sold into Egypt.
The Tree of Life imagery connects to John's apocalyptic vision of the tree bearing twelve fruits.
Nephi's vision of the virgin bearing the Son of God is fulfilled in the annunciation to Mary.
The loss of plain and precious truths is elaborated further in Nephi's later writings.
The great and abominable church parallels the whore of Babylon in John's Revelation.
Nephi recounts the parting of the Red Sea to chasten his brothers' lack of faith.
Alma later quotes the same prophet Zenos on prayer and the Atonement.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 48 nearly verbatim from the brass plates.
Nephi quotes Isaiah 49, the prophecy of the Lord's servant and Israel's gathering.
Nephi's millennial prophecy connects to Christ's own words to the Nephites about the last days.
Alma's discourse on justice and mercy expands the Fall/Atonement framework Lehi introduces here.
Adam and Eve's declaration of joy in the Fall parallels Lehi's teaching that men exist to have joy.
The prophecy of a choice seer is fulfilled in Joseph Smith's calling as described in D&C.
Amulek's teaching on the infinite and eternal sacrifice echoes Jacob's discourse on the infinite atonement.
Joseph Smith provides the inspired interpretation of the stem of Jesse and the root of Jesse.
The relationship between grace and works is a central doctrinal connection between Nephi and Paul.
The encounter with the learned man (Martin Harris and Charles Anthon) fulfills this prophecy.
Nephi's earlier vision of the great and abominable church connects to these latter-day deceptions.
Christ personally teaches the same doctrine of Christ that Nephi outlines here.
Alma later echoes the principle that the word of God has more power than the sword.
The sign of Christ's birth parallels the nativity account in Luke.
The destructions in the Americas coincide with the crucifixion events in Jerusalem.
Christ invites people to feel His wounds, paralleling His appearance to Thomas.
Christ delivers the Sermon at the Temple, closely paralleling the Sermon on the Mount.
Continuation of the Sermon paralleling Matthew 6 on prayer and fasting.
Conclusion of the Sermon paralleling Matthew 7 on judgment and foundations.
Christ identifies the Nephites as the 'other sheep' He spoke of in John.
The Abrahamic covenant that all families of the earth shall be blessed.
Christ quotes Isaiah 54 on the expansion and peace of Zion.
Christ commands the Nephites to record Malachi 3 on tithing and the Lord's messenger.
The promise of Elijah is fulfilled in Moroni's visit to Joseph Smith and the Kirtland Temple.
The naming of Christ's Church is reiterated in the modern-day revelation.
The desire of the Three Nephites parallels the tradition about John the Beloved.
The Nephite Zion society of 'all things common' parallels the early Christian church in Jerusalem.
The elimination of all -ites fulfills Nephi's teaching that God invites all to come unto Him regardless of distinction.
Ammaron instructs Mormon to retrieve the records, directly connecting to the opening of the book of Mormon.
The discovery of the people of Zarahemla and the Mulekite population is further detailed in Mosiah.
Coriantumr's discovery connects the Jaredite civilization to the Nephite record.
Amulek's detailed teaching on prayer echoes Enos's model of persistent, heartfelt supplication.
The witness received after the trial of faith parallels Moroni's teaching on faith preceding evidence.
The promise of receiving truth through sincere prayer connects to Moroni's closing invitation.
The worth of souls and the joy of bringing them to Christ parallels Enos's expanding circle of concern.
Nephi's original statement about making the small plates for a wise purpose is fulfilled in Mormon's discovery.
The Lord's wise purpose for the small plates is revealed when the 116 lost pages are replaced by this record.
Mormon's bridge narrative connects directly to the opening of Mosiah and King Benjamin's reign.
King Benjamin's sermon on caring for the poor expands Jacob's teaching to think of brethren as yourselves.
The law of consecration echoes Jacob's teaching to seek the kingdom of God before riches.
Paul's teaching on Israel's blindness parallels Jacob's concept of looking beyond the mark.
Isaiah's vineyard parable uses similar imagery of God's care for Israel and their failure to produce good fruit.
Paul's olive tree metaphor of grafting Gentiles into Israel parallels Zenos's allegory directly.
Korihor's confrontation with Alma mirrors the Sherem-Jacob encounter as a pattern of anti-Christ opposition.
The Tower of Babel account parallels the Jaredite departure narrative.
The barge construction echoes Noah's ark as a vessel of divine deliverance.
The pre-mortal Christ appearing in bodily form connects to the doctrine that God has a tangible body.
The Jaredite ocean crossing parallels the Lehite voyage in pattern and purpose.
Secret combinations are traced back to Cain's covenant with Satan.
The Gadianton robbers followed the same pattern of secret oaths from Cain.
The faith chapter in Ether parallels and complements the faith chapter in Hebrews.
The promise that weakness becomes strength through grace connects to the promise of the Holy Ghost's constant companionship.
The New Jerusalem prophecy parallels John's vision in Revelation.
The final Jaredite battle at Ramah/Cumorah foreshadows the final Nephite battle at the same location.
The 'inasmuch as ye keep the commandments ye shall prosper' promise is reiterated.
Building on the rock of the Redeemer connects to the baptismal covenant in 2 Nephi.
Nephi cites Moses lifting up the serpent as a type of Christ, as does Jesus to Nicodemus.
The sealing power given to Nephi parallels the keys given to Peter.
Mormon's commentary on man's frailty echoes the Psalmist's 'He knoweth our frame.'
Samuel's rebuke about rejecting prophets mirrors Jesus's lament over Jerusalem.
The new star at Christ's birth parallels the star followed by the Wise Men.
Ammaron's charge to Mormon directly follows the record-keeping chain from 4 Nephi.
The sorrowing of the damned vs godly sorrow parallels Paul's teaching on true repentance.
Mormon's refusal to lead connects to the principle that vengeance belongs to the Lord.
Mormon's lament at Cumorah echoes the final Jaredite destruction at the same location.
Mormon's testimony of the Bible and Book of Mormon working together fulfills Nephi's vision.
Moroni's description of latter-day pride echoes Nephi's earlier prophecies of the last days.
The unchangeable nature of God parallels the Hebrews declaration of Christ's consistency.
Moroni records the words Christ spoke about conferring the Holy Ghost.
The sacramental prayer is repeated verbatim in modern revelation.
The prayer on the wine is repeated verbatim in D&C.
Mormon's discourse on charity closely parallels Paul's chapter on love.
The salvation of little children is confirmed in Joseph Smith's celestial vision.
Moroni's list of spiritual gifts parallels Paul's enumeration.
The modern revelation on spiritual gifts expands on Moroni's and Paul's teachings.
Benjamin's teaching on serving God through service to others parallels Christ's sheep and goats parable.
The angel's description of Christ's birth parallels Luke's nativity account.
The role of a seer as described by Ammon is confirmed in modern revelation.
Abinadi quotes the Ten Commandments directly from the brass plates.
Abinadi quotes Isaiah 53 on the suffering servant.
Abinadi's Christology connects to the modern revelation on the nature of Christ.
The baptismal covenant at the Waters of Mormon parallels the modern baptismal requirements.
The Lord easing burdens connects to Alma's later prayer for strength under affliction.
Alma the Younger's conversion account is retold in greater detail in Alma 36.
The reign of judges begins in Alma 1 with the immediate challenges Alma faces.
Alma refers to his own conversion and spiritual rebirth.
Prophecy of Christ bearing our griefs and sorrows.
Universal nature of the resurrection.
The account of Melchizedek as a high priest.
The definition of faith parallels Paul's formulation.
The Liahona's original description and discovery.
The necessity of opposition and the law in the plan of redemption.
The call of Abraham to leave his country.
Those who are Christ's are Abraham's seed and heirs.
Intelligence was not created or made.
Parallel creation account.
Joseph Smith's prayer fulfills James's invitation to ask of God.
The recorded words of John the Baptist when conferring the Aaronic Priesthood.
Prophecy of Elijah's return quoted by Moroni with slight variations.
Joseph Smith—Matthew is a more correct translation and expansion of Matthew 24.
The Lord gives a similar revelation concerning the signs of His coming to Joseph Smith in 1831.
References the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel the prophet.
Both passages describe Christ as the means of salvation and the purpose of God's work.
Moses 2 is the revealed restoration of the Genesis creation account.
Both texts describe the premortal council and Satan's rebellion.
Links the Fall of Adam to the necessary conditions for human existence and joy.
Describes a society living in a Zion-like state of unity and peace.
Both passages define the nature of the Godhead.
Nephi outlines the same first principles and ordinances.
Expands on the gathering of Israel and the New Jerusalem.
Both texts describe Josiah's Passover celebration.
Opening verses are nearly identical regarding Cyrus's decree.
Both provide lists of Jewish families returning from captivity.
Introduces Ezra and his genealogy.
Both describe the public reading of the Law.
Antiochus setting up the abomination of desolation fulfills Daniel's prophecy.
Mattathias's zeal compared to Phinehas.
Trust in God to deliver the many by the few echoes Jonathan's faith.
The NT mentions the Feast of Dedication established here.
Every man under his vine and fig tree mirrors the prophetic vision of peace.
The imagery of a hen gathering her chickens is used by both the Lord in Esdras and Jesus in the New Testament.
The eagle vision in Esdras parallels Daniel's vision of the fourth beast with many horns.
The Man from the sea destroying the wicked parallels Christ's coming in the book of Revelation.
Both passages discuss the scattering and eventual gathering of the lost tribes of Israel.
The concept of sealed or reserved scriptures for the wise parallels instructions given in the Restoration.
Both texts emphasize the literal nature of the resurrection of the body.
Both books record the cleansing of the temple by Judas Maccabeus.
The practice of offering for the dead relates to the LDS doctrine of work for the dead.
The vision of deceased prophets looking after the living parallels the LDS view of the spirit world.
Refers to the light and joy that came to the Jews after their deliverance.
Both texts emphasize that God does whatever He pleases because of His supreme power.
Esther's recognition of her weakness and need for God's grace parallels the themes of humility in the Book of Mormon.
The king's heart being turned by God is a literal example of the proverb.
The formal record of the king's decree allowing the Jews to defend themselves.
Both texts use near-identical language to confess that righteousness belongs to God while shame belongs to Israel.
The horrors mentioned here are the fulfillment of the curses of the law for breaking the covenant.
Christian tradition often links the wisdom conversing with men to the incarnation of Jesus Christ.
Both describe the gathering of the children of Israel from the four quarters of the earth.
Both passages describe Jeremiah sending a letter to the captives in Babylon.
This serves as a second account of Daniel being cast into a den of lions for his faith.
Both passages contrast the worship of man-made idols with the worship of the living God.
Daniel's prayer echoes the theme that the Lord does not forsake those who seek Him.
Both texts emphasize the turning to God through fasting and sackcloth during times of crisis.
Achior's statement about prosperity contingent on righteousness matches the promise given to the Nephites.
Judith references the zeal of Simeon and Levi in avenging the defilement of their sister Dinah.
The beheading of Holofernes by Judith is a literary and thematic parallel to Jael killing Sisera.
Both Judith and David use the enemy's own sword to decapitate their respective oppressors.
This book is an addition to the narrative of the three Hebrews in the fiery furnace found in Daniel.
Both passages acknowledge God's justice in punishing Israel for their disobedience.
The 'Song of the Three Holy Children' parallels the structure and themes of Psalm 148, calling all creation to praise God.
This prayer is the traditional text of the prayer mentioned in the historical account of Manasseh's repentance while in Babylonian captivity.
Both passages emphasize the Lord's willingness to forgive those who confess their sins with a humble heart.
Manasseh's 'bowing the knee of the heart' parallels Alma the Younger's internal cry for mercy during his intense repentance.
Shared wisdom theme regarding the Fear of the Lord as the beginning of knowledge.
Refinement theme (gold tried in the fire) as a reason for trials.
Core doctrine of moral agency and the choice between life and death.
Logos/Wisdom theology regarding the pre-mortal role of the Word in creation.
Mandate to forgive others to be forgiven by God.
God's special attention to the prayer and status of the poor.
Descriptions of the beauty and liturgical function of the Aaronic priestly garments.
Susanna's refusal to sin against God mirrors Joseph's response to Potiphar's wife.
The execution of the elders follows the Mosaic law that a false witness receives the punishment they intended for the accused.
Strong parallels in the doctrinal emphasis on almsgiving and caring for the poor as a spiritual necessity.
Parallels regarding the sanctity and purpose of the marriage relationship.
Common imagery of angels presenting the prayers of the saints before the throne of God.
Descriptions of the New Jerusalem built with gold and precious stones.
Striking parallel to the mockery of Jesus Christ on the cross regarding being the Son of God.
The peaceful state of the righteous after death.
The Armor of God imagery used to describe divine attributes turned to military purpose.
Description of Christ (Wisdom) as the brightness of God's glory and the express image of His person.
The concept of the mortal body as a tabernacle or burden that weighs down the soul.
Refers to the brazen serpent as a sign of salvation where healing comes from looking toward God.
Refers to the gift of Manna as angels' food.
The Natural Man — both passages teach about the natural man's inability to receive spiritual things.
Glory of the faithful — unimaginable things God has prepared for those who love Him.
Body as temple — the Spirit of God dwells in the Saints.
Institution of the Sacrament — parallel accounts of the Lord's Supper.
Creation of woman — neither man nor woman is complete without the other in the Lord.
Baptism for the dead — the primary biblical evidence for proxy ordinances.
Degrees of Glory — celestial, terrestrial, and telestial kingdoms.
Universal resurrection — as in Adam all die, so in Christ shall all be made alive.
Both texts emphasize the power of the Atonement to cleanse those who return to the Lord.
LDS scripture provides a direct parallel to Christ's role as our Advocate with the Father.
Mormon echoes John's teaching that we must have pure love to be like Christ when He appears.
The Joseph Smith Translation clarifies that God is seen by those who have faith and the Spirit.
Confirms the unity of the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost as one Godhead.
Peter quotes the Old Testament command to be holy.
Quotation regarding the transience of flesh and endurance of the Word.
The prophecy of the rejected stone becoming the head of the corner.
Reference to Israel being a kingdom of priests.
LDS revelation expanding on Christ's ministry in the spirit world.
The principle that love covers a multitude of sins.
Peter cites the teaching that God gives grace to the humble.
Both passages speak of the anticipation of the Savior's return from heaven.
Description of the Lord's descent with the sound of a trump to gather the elect.
Prophecy of the Saints being caught up to meet the Lord at His coming.
The specific imagery of the day of the Lord coming as a thief in the night.
Both texts emphasize the necessity of praying without ceasing in all circumstances.
Both passages identify Jesus as the mediator of the new covenant.
Both references describe the conditions of apostasy in the latter days.
Attributes of a believer—charity, faith, and purity—are reinforced.
Paul warns of the love of money, while Jacob provides the proper perspective on seeking riches for the intent to do good.
Both speak of being sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise as an earnest of our inheritance.
The Lord's promise to Joseph Smith that small moments of affliction are but for a moment and will be for his good.
Both describe the reality of all people standing before the bar of God to be judged for their works.
Alma explains to Corianton that only godly sorrow for sin can bring about the repentance that leads to salvation.
Joseph Smith explains how to discern if a messenger is from God or is Satan appearing as an angel of light.
Paul's reference to the third heaven corresponds to the vision of the degrees of glory given to Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon.
Moroni records that the Lord makes weak things become strong through His grace, echoing Paul's experience with his thorn in the flesh.
Both passages define love for God as the keeping of His commandments.
John provides the same criteria for discerning the spirit of antichrist: the denial of Christ's physical manifestation.
Both texts emphasize the necessity of following the doctrine of Christ to attain salvation.
Connects Peter's exhortation to make calling and election sure with Joseph Smith's teachings on the more sure word of prophecy.
A parallel account of the Father's voice at the Transfiguration.
Reflects the measurement of time in Kolob, where one day is as a thousand years.
Both passages describe the elements melting and the earth being renewed at Christ's coming.
Both passages describe the Lord's presence and the fire that attends His coming to judge the world.
Paul's prophecy of the falling away aligns with the Old Testament prophecy of a famine of hearing the words of the Lord.
The Lord's command in the Restoration that the idler shall not eat the bread of the laborer mirrors Paul's instruction.
John uses nearly identical language to express joy in those walking in truth.
Both passages emphasize that doing good is associated with God while evil leads to a loss of spiritual sight.
The reference to Jannes and Jambres links to the Exodus account of the Egyptian magicians who withstood Moses.
Descriptions of the last days mirror Book of Mormon prophecies.
Both describe spiritual conditions of the latter days and the need for restoration.
Duty of Elders is to be ordained as witnesses of the resurrection.
The doctrine of Christ regarding baptism and the gift of the Holy Ghost.
Regarding the restitution of all things spoken of by the mouth of prophets.
God is no respecter of persons; he is the same yesterday, today, and forever.
Mankind being the offspring of God; intelligence not created or made.
Christ as creator of all things parallels the Pearl of Great Price account of God creating worlds without number.
Burial and resurrection through baptism parallels the covenant of obedience described in the vision of the celestial kingdom.
Setting affections on things above echoes the command to lift up hearts and be glad.
Speaking with grace seasoned with salt parallels the instruction to let speech be seasoned with wisdom.
The gathering together of all things in Christ parallels the welding link connecting dispensations described in D&C.
The church built upon the foundation of apostles and prophets directly parallels the sixth Article of Faith on church organization.
The offices of apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, and teachers parallel the priesthood offices described in D&C.
Justification by faith in Christ rather than works of the law parallels the Book of Mormon teaching that we are saved by grace after all we can do.
The law as a schoolmaster to bring Israel to Christ parallels Abinadi's teaching that the law of Moses was given to point toward Christ.
The fruit of the Spirit parallels Alma's seed metaphor where good fruit grows from a seed of faith nourished by the word.
Whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap parallels the law of restoration — wickedness never was happiness.
Connects Christ being the express image of the Father to His role as the Firstborn.
Uses the specific language of called of God as was Aaron to define priesthood authority.
Clarifies that it is the Priesthood that is without father/mother/beginning, not the man Melchizedek himself.
Correlates the definition of faith as not having a perfect knowledge but hoping for things not seen which are true.
Confirms God as the Father of spirits who stood in the midst of the intelligences before the world was.
Joseph Smith's foundational experience in the Sacred Grove was prompted by reading James 1:5.
Amulek teaches that prayer without works is vain, paralleling James's teaching that faith without works is dead.
The Lord provides a similar pattern for healing the sick through priesthood administration and faith.

references.justification: The JST provides critical restoration context to the prologue, clarifying the roles of the Son and John the Baptist.
The doctrine of being born again of water and Spirit was taught to Adam, paralleling Jesus' words to Nicodemus.
Jesus explicitly identifies the Nephites in the Americas as the other sheep He mentioned in Jerusalem.
Abinadi quotes the commandment to keep the Sabbath and not make images, but the focus on love fulfills the intent of the law.
Modern revelation confirms the account in John 21 that the apostle John was translated and allowed to tarry on earth.
Jude's mention of the first estate is a direct link to the LDS doctrine of pre-mortal life and the war in heaven.
Enoch's prophecy of the Lord coming with ten thousands of his saints is elaborated upon in the Book of Moses.
The themes of false teachers, the fall of angels, and the judgment of the ungodly are nearly identical in these two epistles.
Explains the preparatory nature of the Aaronic Priesthood which John the Baptist was sent to restore.
Clarifies that Jesus was not just listening to the doctors but that they were hearing and asking Him questions.
Parallels John's teaching on the necessity of baptism by water and the Spirit.
The messianic prophecy that Jesus explicitly quotes and claims to fulfill in Nazareth.
The resurrected Savior gives a similar sermon at the temple in Bountiful.
Amulek teaches how mercy can satisfy the demands of justice, illustrated in the forgiveness of the woman.
Reveals that the earth will be transfigured like the Savior was on the mount.
Defines the office of the Seventy in the modern church, patterned after the mission in Luke 10.
The Lord uses the same imagery of a hen gathering her chickens when speaking to the Nephites.
Confirms that the Lord's joy is great in the soul that repenteth.
Nephi exhorts us to pray always and not faint, echoing the Savior's parable.
Clarifies the Savior's teaching on marriage in the resurrection.
Joseph Smith's inspired revision of the parallel prophecy in Matthew provides extensive detail on the signs of the times.
The Savior's personal description of the suffering in Gethsemane which Luke records as drops of blood.
Alma prophesies of the specific physical and spiritual pains the Savior would take upon Himself.
Parallels the witness of the resurrected body where disciples felt the wounds in His hands and feet.
Mark explicitly identifies John the Baptist as the fulfillment of this prophecy.
The practice of anointing the sick with oil is confirmed in modern revelation.
Modern revelation confirms the Transfiguration and explains its significance.
Expands on the teaching that marriage joined by God is intended to endure beyond mortality.
Joseph Smith's inspired revision provides extensive detail on the signs of the times.
The Savior's personal description of the suffering in Gethsemane.
Moroni records the same instructions and promises regarding the signs that follow those who believe.

note: The birth of Jesus to a virgin fulfills Isaiah's prophecy of Immanuel.
Bethlehem identified as the birthplace of the Messiah per Micah's prophecy.
Jesus quotes Deuteronomy in resisting Satan's first temptation.
The Sermon on the Mount is repeated to the Nephites with the added phrase 'even as I, or your Father who is in heaven is perfect.'
The principle of losing one's life to find it is echoed in modern revelation.
The mustard seed parable parallels Alma's teaching of the word as a seed that grows into a mighty tree.
The keys of the kingdom given to Peter are referenced in the context of sealing authority.
Moses and Elias (Elijah) appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration and later in the Kirtland Temple.
The rejected cornerstone prophecy is explored in depth by the prophet Jacob.
Joseph Smith's inspired revision of the Olivet discourse clarifies the signs of the Second Coming.
The atoning blood shed for the remission of sins is a central theme shared by King Benjamin.
Jesus quotes David's messianic psalm from the cross.
Onesimus is mentioned alongside Tychicus as a faithful brother being sent to the Colossians.
In Christ there is neither bond nor free — the theological basis for Paul's appeal.
The command to esteem every man as yourself echoes Paul's request to receive Onesimus as himself.
Christ's condescension and self-emptying is shown in Nephi's vision of the Son of God descending to minister among men.
The thirteenth Article of Faith directly echoes Paul's admonition to seek after virtuous, lovely, and praiseworthy things.
Both passages describe the glorified, resurrected Christ appearing to prophets.
D&C 77 provides direct, revealed answers to John's questions about the symbols in Revelation 4.
The sealed book is identified as containing God's will and the history of the earth in its seven thousand years.
The 144,000 are identified as high priests ordained to administer the everlasting gospel.
The little book John ate is identified as a mission to gather Israel in the last days.
Moses provides the pre-mortal context for Satan's rebellion and the War in Heaven.
Both passages describe the angel flying through the midst of heaven with the everlasting gospel.
The marriage of the Lamb is illuminated by the doctrine of celestial/eternal marriage.
Both describe conditions during the Millennium, including peace and the binding of Satan.
Ether's vision of the New Jerusalem parallels John's, identifying it as a holy city for the righteous.
Paul quotes Habakkuk's declaration that the just shall live by faith as the thesis of his epistle.
Both passages teach the universal fallen state of mankind and the necessity of divine grace.
Both passages explain the Fall of Adam and its effects upon all humanity, with redemption through Christ.
Joint-heirs with Christ inherit the celestial kingdom, receiving all that the Father hath.
Both passages develop the relationship between faith, hearing the word, and spiritual growth.
The Allegory of the Olive Tree in Jacob is the most extensive parallel to Paul's olive tree metaphor.
Both passages call believers to consecrate themselves wholly to God through Christ's grace.
Both passages describe the character qualifications and righteous conduct required of priesthood leaders.
Both teach that salvation comes through grace, with the expectation of faithful effort on the believer's part.
Both passages describe the spiritual rebirth and transformation that comes through God's mercy, not man's merit.
Both teach that the voice of God's servants is the same as His own voice.
Moroni quotes Malachi's prophecy about Elijah with slight modifications for the Restoration context.
Mormon's insertion of the small plates connects directly to the lost 116 pages crisis.
The list of virtues in verse 6 closely parallels Peter's ladder of Christian attributes.
Moroni's prophecy that three witnesses would see the plates is fulfilled through this revelation.
Christ identifies Himself using the same language as John's Gospel—'the light which shineth in darkness.'
Clarifies the New Testament account of Jesus's statement about John tarrying until He comes.
Moses parting the Red Sea is cited as an example of the spirit of revelation in action.
Builds on the previous revelation's teaching about receiving knowledge in mind and heart.
Both sections address the same crisis—the lost 116 manuscript pages—with complementary solutions.
Both passages define Christ's church as simply those who repent and come unto Him.
Later revelation confirms John the Baptist's role in restoring the Aaronic Priesthood.
The principle of establishing truth through multiple witnesses echoes Paul's teaching.
The Savior's parable of the lost sheep teaches the same principle about the worth of every soul.
King Benjamin's angel likewise prophesied that Christ would bleed at every pore from anguish.
Luke records Jesus sweating drops of blood in Gethsemane, paralleling this firsthand account.
The sacrament bread prayer is nearly identical to the one recorded in the Book of Mormon.
The sacrament water prayer matches the Book of Mormon ordinance language.
Both teach that the prophet's words should be received as if from God's own mouth.
Emma called 'elect lady' echoes John's epistle addressing 'the elect lady and her children.'
Both celebrate sacred song as prayer and worship pleasing to God.
Christ directly invokes Paul's armor of God imagery—loins girt with truth, breastplate of righteousness, shield of faith.
Both describe Satan drawing a third part of heaven's hosts away through rebellion.
Both teach that little children are redeemed and cannot sin because they lack accountability.
Both describe Christ dwelling with the righteous on earth for a thousand years.
Both reference the city of Enoch (Zion) being taken into God's bosom.
Christ's command to 'be one' echoes His intercessory prayer that His followers may be one as He and the Father are one.
Both passages command baptism to 'wash away your sins, calling on my name.'
James Covill's story mirrors the Parable of the Sower—receiving the word with gladness but falling away due to persecution and worldly cares.
Both teach that looking upon a woman with lust is a sin of the heart equivalent to adultery.
'Inasmuch as ye impart... ye will do it unto me' echoes Christ's teaching about serving the least of these.
Both promise progressive revelation—knowledge upon knowledge, light growing brighter unto the perfect day.
Christ recounts and expands His Olivet Discourse from Matthew 24 with additional Restoration details.
The Jews asking 'What are these wounds?' fulfills Zechariah's prophecy about wounds in the house of friends.
The parable of the ten virgins applied—the wise who took the Holy Spirit for their guide shall abide the day.
Paul's teaching on gifts of the Spirit closely parallels this D&C enumeration of spiritual gifts.
Affirms marriage as ordained of God and that 'they twain shall be one flesh,' correcting Shaker celibacy.
Both describe the path of the righteous as a light that grows brighter and brighter unto the perfect day.
Both teach that men should be actively engaged in good works and not require compulsion in all things.
Christ restates the two great commandments—love God with all your heart, and love your neighbor as yourself.
Both identify a broken heart and contrite spirit as the sacrifice acceptable to God.
Both teach that a wicked and adulterous generation seeks after signs; sign-seeking is condemned.
Both command unconditional forgiveness—'I, the Lord, will forgive whom I will forgive, but of you it is required to forgive all men.'
Directly references Daniel's prophecy of a stone cut without hands that fills the earth—the kingdom of God rolling forth.
Both teach that when men speak moved by the Holy Ghost, their words are scripture/prophecy.
Both command parents to teach their children to walk in truth, pray, and keep the commandments.
Both describe a vision of the Son of God on the right hand of the Father—Stephen in Acts, Joseph and Sidney here.
Both testify that through Christ the worlds were made and the inhabitants are begotten sons and daughters of God.
Paul's teaching on the glory of the sun, moon, and stars directly parallels the three degrees of glory described here.
Directly echoes: 'Unto whom much is given much is required; and he who sins against the greater light shall receive the greater condemnation.'
The oath and covenant of the priesthood—those who magnify their callings are sanctified and receive the Father.
Both address the Book of Mormon as containing the fulness of the gospel; the church is under condemnation for neglecting it.
D&C interpretation of the parable of the wheat and tares from Matthew's Gospel.
Fulfilled 29 years later when South Carolina seceded, beginning the Civil War—one of the most specific D&C prophecies.
Both describe Christ as the true light that lighteth every man who comes into the world.
Both command seeking learning by study and also by faith—a foundational LDS educational principle.
Both teach that intelligence was not created or made and is co-eternal with God.
'The glory of God is intelligence, or, in other words, light and truth'—parallels John's teaching on the Word full of grace and truth.
The temple dedication prayer (Section 109) fulfills the command given here to build a temple for an endowment of power.
Both promise that those with pure hearts and clean hands shall stand in the Lord's holy place and see God.
Both command loving enemies and praying for persecutors—the law of peace and forgiveness.
The parable of the nobleman and the olive trees echoes Isaiah's vineyard song about Israel.
The Adam-ondi-Ahman council where Adam blesses his posterity parallels Daniel's Ancient of Days vision.
Joseph petitions for the Spirit to be poured out upon all flesh, echoing Joel's prophecy.
Christ's sudden appearance in the Kirtland Temple fulfills 'the Lord, whom ye seek, shall suddenly come to his temple.'
Elijah's appearance fulfills the prophecy of his return before the great and dreadful day, and D&C 2.
Both establish that the church must be called by Christ's name to be His church.
Both teach the principle of tithing—returning a portion to the Lord—with promises of blessing.
Both teach about priesthood authority and the conditions under which it operates—righteousness, persuasion, and love.
Both teach that Christ descended below all things and understands human suffering perfectly.
The welding link between fathers and children fulfills Malachi's prophecy about Elijah turning hearts.
Both teach about sealing power—what is bound on earth being bound in heaven.
Christ's appearance in red apparel directly quotes Isaiah's imagery of the Lord treading the winepress alone.
Joseph and Hyrum's martyrdom echoes Christ's teaching that greater love hath no man than to lay down his life for his friends.
Both teach that the Lord looketh on the heart—God judges by desires, not appearances.
Both describe Christ preaching to the spirits in prison, with D&C 138 expanding the doctrine significantly.
Mentions that Judah prevailed above his brethren and from him came the chief ruler.
Identifies David as the one who shall feed the Lord's people Israel.
The common declaration of thanksgiving: give thanks to the Lord for his mercy endureth forever.
Quoted in the New Testament regarding the Sonship of Christ.
Promise regarding the building of the house and the eternal establishment of the throne.
Echoes the Davidic covenant promise that his sons will sit on the throne if they walk faithfully.
Parallel account of God granting Solomon wisdom along with riches and honor.
Stephen quotes Solomon's acknowledgment that God does not dwell in temples made with hands.
Solomon's declaration that there is no man who does not sin echoes the universal sinfulness of humanity.
Jesus references the Queen of the South who came to hear Solomon's wisdom.
James cites Elijah's prayer that shut the heavens as an example of effective fervent prayer.
Women received their dead raised to life again, referencing the widow of Zarephath.
The call to choose between God and Baal parallels Christ's teaching that no one can serve two masters.
Paul quotes Elijah's complaint about Israel forsaking God's covenant and killing the prophets.
Paul quotes God's response to Elijah about the seven thousand who have not bowed to Baal.
Elijah's prophecy about Ahab's blood is fulfilled in the judgment upon his son Joram.
Hannah's song of praise closely parallels Mary's Magnificat in its themes of divine reversal and exaltation of the humble.
Both passages warn of the dangers when a people rejects divine governance in favor of earthly kingship.
Both emphasize that God desires obedience and mercy over ritual sacrifice.
Both teach that God judges by the heart, not by outward appearance.
Both declare that the battle is the Lord's, not man's.
Jesus cites David eating the showbread as a precedent for mercy over rigid law observance.
David's refusal to harm the Lord's anointed reflects the principle taught in Psalms.
Chronicles interprets Saul's death as divine judgment for consulting a medium instead of the Lord.
Mount Moriah as a sacred site connected to the temple and the concept of a holy city.
The declaration that the Lord is good and his mercy endureth forever.
The recognition that God's presence can dwell in a temple built by mortal hands.
Fire and glory filling a dedicated house of the Lord.
The promise that humility and prayer lead to forgiveness and healing of the land.
Encouragement to be strong and not let hands be weak, for work shall be rewarded.
The battle is not yours but God's—trust in divine deliverance.
Discovery of sacred scripture that transforms national direction, paralleling Nephi's encounter with divine records.
Cyrus's decree to rebuild the temple in Jerusalem, repeated nearly verbatim at the opening of Ezra.
James and John allude to Elijah calling down fire from heaven.
Elijah's translation foreshadows his prophesied return before the great day of the Lord.
Elijah appears with Moses at the Transfiguration, confirming his continued ministry.
Elisha's raising of the Shunammite's son mirrors Elijah's raising of the widow's son.
Jesus cites Naaman's healing to show God's mercy extends to Gentiles.
The exile of Israel fulfills Amos' prophecy of captivity beyond Damascus.
Hezekiah destroys the bronze serpent that Moses had made, which had become an idol.
Jesus references Moses lifting up the serpent as a type of His own crucifixion.
Parallel account of the angel destroying the Assyrian army.
The discovered Book of the Law likely corresponds to the scroll Moses placed beside the Ark.
Josiah fulfills the Shema by turning to the Lord with all his heart, soul, and might.
Parallel account of the burning of the temple and the great houses of Jerusalem.
The exile fulfills Jeremiah's prophecy of seventy years of Babylonian captivity.
References the book of Jasher, also cited in Joshua's account of the sun standing still.
The anointing of David as shepherd and captain over Israel is recorded in both accounts.
Peter cites this Davidic covenant promise as fulfilled in the resurrection of Christ.
Quoted in the New Testament regarding the divine Sonship of Christ.
Matthew's genealogy of Jesus deliberately mentions Bathsheba as 'her that had been the wife of Uriah.'
Psalm 51 is David's penitential psalm composed after Nathan's confrontation.
This psalm is virtually identical to Psalm 18, David's song of deliverance.
David testifies the Spirit of the Lord spoke by him, illustrating how holy men spoke as moved by the Holy Ghost.
David's refusal to offer burnt offerings without cost is recorded in both accounts.
The invitation to reason together and be cleansed from sin parallels the call to repent and not procrastinate.
The mountain of the Lord's house established in the top of the mountains, with nations flowing unto it.
Nephi's quotation of Isaiah's throne-room vision in the Book of Mormon.
The Immanuel prophecy of a virgin bearing a son parallels Nephi's vision of the mother of the Son of God.
The messianic prophecy of the child born, Wonderful Counselor, Prince of Peace, quoted directly by Nephi.
The stem of Jesse identified in latter-day revelation as Christ and the priesthood connection.
The fall of Lucifer parallels the vision of the sons of perdition and Satan's pre-mortal rebellion.
The earth defiled by inhabitants who have broken the everlasting covenant parallels the declaration of universal apostasy.
Line upon line, precept upon precept — the pattern of progressive revelation.
The sealed book echoes the episode of Martin Harris taking the characters to Professor Anthon.
The angel of the Lord destroys the Assyrian army, demonstrating God's power to deliver His people.
The voice crying in the wilderness to prepare the way of the Lord, applied to John the Baptist.
Every knee shall bow and every tongue confess — a universal acknowledgment of God's sovereignty.
Can a woman forget her sucking child? Nephi quotes this passage about God's unfailing love for Zion.
Awake and put on thy strength, O Zion — interpreted in the Doctrine and Covenants regarding the last days.
How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that publisheth peace — quoted by Abinadi.
He is despised and rejected of men, a man of sorrows — Abinadi quotes the entire chapter.
God's ways are higher than man's ways — a theme echoed in Nephi's trust in divine purposes.
Jesus reads this passage in the synagogue at Nazareth, declaring it fulfilled in their hearing.
Imagery of garments red from treading the winepress — applied to Christ's second coming.
Jesus quotes this verse to prove the resurrection, identifying God as the God of the living.
Jesus echoes the divine I AM when He declares 'Before Abraham was, I am.'
The Passover lamb's unbroken bones foreshadow that none of Christ's bones were broken at the crucifixion.
Jesus identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven, fulfilling the type of manna.
Paul identifies the rock that gave water as Christ: 'that Rock was Christ.'
Peter applies the 'kingdom of priests and holy nation' language to the church.
Paul quotes the fifth commandment as the first commandment with a promise.
The blood of the covenant at Sinai is a type of the blood of the new covenant in Christ.
The author of Hebrews quotes the command to make all things according to the heavenly pattern.
Paul echoes Moses' willingness to be blotted out for the sake of his people.
John affirms that no man has seen God but that the Son has revealed Him.
The psalmist echoes God's self-proclaimed character: merciful, gracious, and slow to anger.
Both passages establish God as the Creator of all things from the beginning.
The Pearl of Great Price provides a parallel creation account with additional detail.
Jesus quotes this verse on the divine institution of marriage.
The protevangelium connects to the eschatological defeat of the serpent.
The Pearl of Great Price provides a parallel account of the curse upon Adam.
Both passages address the progression of sin when temptation is entertained.
The New Testament explains that Enoch was translated because he pleased God.
Peter references the ark as a type of salvation through water.
The JST expands the rainbow covenant with additional context about the last days.
Paul quotes the Abrahamic covenant promise that all nations will be blessed.
Melchizedek's priesthood is a type of Christ's eternal priesthood.
Paul quotes this verse as the foundational text on justification by faith.
Paul references the renaming of Abraham as father of many nations.
The binding of Isaac is cited as Abraham's supreme act of faith.
Peter quotes this Abrahamic covenant promise regarding the Messiah.
Jesus references Jacob's ladder as a type of Himself as the connection between heaven and earth.
The scepter of Judah and Shiloh prophecy find fulfillment in Christ, the Lion of Judah.
Joseph's declaration that God meant evil for good parallels Paul's teaching on divine providence.
The scapegoat bearing the sins of the people foreshadows the Suffering Servant upon whom the Lord laid the iniquity of us all.
The life of the flesh is in the blood parallels the New Testament teaching that without shedding of blood there is no remission.
Love thy neighbour as thyself is quoted by Jesus as the second great commandment.
The Passover lamb parallels Christ our passover sacrificed for us.
I will walk among you and be your God is quoted by Paul regarding the temple of the living God.
The image of the rose and the lily echoes Isaiah's vision of the desert blossoming.
The banner of love over the beloved parallels Christ's love for the church.
The garden enclosed and fountain sealed parallel the imagery of marital fidelity in Proverbs.
Love as strong as death and unquenchable by many waters parallels the enduring nature of charity.
Nothing can overcome love, echoing Paul's declaration that nothing can separate us from the love of God.
The Ten Commandments restated, paralleling the original giving at Sinai.
Jesus identifies this as the first and great commandment.
Jesus quotes this verse when tempted by Satan in the wilderness.
Peter identifies Christ as the Prophet like Moses foretold here.
Christ himself quotes this Messianic prophecy to the Nephites.
Paul applies the curse of hanging on a tree to Christ's crucifixion.
Paul cites the unmuzzled ox to support the right of ministers to receive sustenance.
Paul applies the accessibility of the word to the nearness of Christ.
Paul quotes the song of Moses on vengeance belonging to God.
Jude references the archangel Michael disputing about the body of Moses.
Paul quotes 'Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated' to illustrate divine election.
Applied to John the Baptist as the messenger sent to prepare the way before the Lord.
The principle of generous giving and receiving abundant blessings in return.
Jesus identifies John the Baptist as the Elijah who was to come before the great day.
Elijah's return fulfilled in the Kirtland Temple when he committed the sealing keys to Joseph Smith.
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge/wisdom — a shared foundational principle.
Trust in the Lord with all thine heart parallels blessed is the man that trusteth in the Lord.
Quoted in Hebrews regarding the Lord's chastening of those he loves.
God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble — quoted directly by James.
Wisdom's presence at creation parallels the Word being with God in the beginning.
Finding wisdom finds life, paralleling Christ as the way, the truth, and the life.
The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, echoing the tree of life in Revelation.
A soft answer turns away wrath parallels being slow to anger in James.
Pride goes before destruction parallels Edom's pride leading to downfall.
Training a child in the way he should go parallels the commandment to teach children the gospel.
If thine enemy hunger feed him — quoted directly by Paul.
Where there is no vision the people perish parallels the famine of hearing the word of the Lord.
The virtuous woman description parallels Boaz calling Ruth a virtuous woman.
Quoted by Paul: Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee.
Quoted in Hebrews regarding Christ's incarnation and exaltation over all things.
Peter quotes this at Pentecost as a prophecy of Christ's resurrection.
Jesus cries from the cross: My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me.
They parted my garments among them and upon my vesture did they cast lots.
The Lord is my shepherd parallels Jesus declaring himself the good shepherd.
He keepeth all his bones: not one of them is broken — fulfilled at the crucifixion.
Sacrifice and offering thou didst not desire; a body hast thou prepared me.
He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me — Judas' betrayal.
Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever — applied to Christ in Hebrews.
Paul quotes David's confession: that thou mightest be justified when thou speakest.
Thou hast ascended on high, thou hast led captivity captive — applied to Christ's ascension.
The zeal of thine house hath eaten me up — applied to Jesus cleansing the temple.
They gave me also gall for my meat and vinegar for my thirst — fulfilled at the crucifixion.
Jesus quotes: I said, ye are gods — in his defense before the Jews.
Satan quotes this psalm during the temptation of Jesus in the wilderness.
Extensively quoted in Hebrews warning against hardening the heart as in the wilderness.
Applied to Christ as creator: Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth.
The most-quoted OT verse in the NT: The LORD said unto my Lord, Sit thou at my right hand.
Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek — central to Hebrews' argument.
The stone which the builders refused is become the head stone of the corner.
Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord — shouted at Jesus' triumphal entry.
The Aaronic blessing echoed in the psalmist's plea for God's face to shine upon His people.
Moses quotes God's self-declaration of mercy and justice from the golden calf incident.
Jesus explicitly compares the bronze serpent lifted up by Moses to His own crucifixion.
Balaam's prophecy of a Star out of Jacob is connected to the star at Christ's birth.
Phinehas' zealous act is praised and counted to him for righteousness.
Moses' concern that Israel not be as sheep without a shepherd echoed by Jesus.
The city of refuge as a type of the refuge believers find in Christ.
The kingdom of God shall break in pieces all other kingdoms and stand forever.
The stone cut out of the mountain without hands rolling forth to fill the whole earth.
When thou walkest through the fire, thou shalt not be burned.
The Son of Man coming in the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.
Dominion and glory and a kingdom given to the Lamb.
Seventy weeks determined to finish the transgression and bring in everlasting righteousness.
Many that sleep in the dust shall awake, some to everlasting life and some to shame.
We brought nothing into this world and can carry nothing out.
Naked came he and naked shall he return.
Paul quotes this verse about God taking the wise in their own craftiness.
The question of whether man shall live again, answered by Christ's resurrection.
Job's declaration of a living Redeemer parallels Isaiah's proclamation of the Lord as Redeemer.
Seeing God in the flesh anticipates the doctrine of bodily resurrection.
God stretches the north over the empty place and hangs the earth upon nothing.
The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom.
God laying the foundations of the earth, paralleling wisdom's role at creation.
The morning stars singing together at creation echoes the premortal council.
Moving from hearing to seeing God parallels Moses' direct experience of God's presence.
James cites the patience of Job and the Lord's compassionate outcome.
The subjection of creation to vanity echoes the Preacher's declaration that all is vanity.
Both passages affirm that times and seasons are in God's hands.
God's eternal nature is set in the heart of man and evident through creation.
Both warn against using many words in prayer; let words before God be few.
The universal sinfulness of mankind: there is not a just man upon earth that doeth good and sinneth not.
Both passages urge wholehearted effort in whatever one's hand finds to do.
The mystery of the wind and of God's works parallels the mystery of the Spirit's movement.
The spirit returns to God who gave it, reversing the creation of man from dust.
The whole duty of man—to fear God and keep his commandments—echoes Moses' charge to Israel.
God will bring every work into judgment, paralleling the judgment seat of Christ.
The four living creatures with multiple faces parallel the four beasts around the throne in Revelation.
Ezekiel is appointed watchman over Israel in both his original commission and recommission after Jerusalem's fall.
Individual righteous prayer and its limits—even the most righteous can only save themselves.
A tender twig planted on a high mountain, paralleling Isaiah's Branch from the root of Jesse.
Each person dies for their own sin; children shall not be put to death for their fathers.
The crown overturned until He comes whose right it is, echoing Shiloh in Jacob's blessing.
God will set up one shepherd over His flock, paralleling Christ as the Good Shepherd.
A new heart and spirit of flesh rather than stone, echoed in Paul's description of hearts as tablets of flesh.
The breath of life entering dead bodies, paralleling the two witnesses standing on their feet.
The stick of Judah and stick of Joseph becoming one, understood in LDS theology as the Bible and Book of Mormon.
God's tabernacle with His people forever, echoing the new Jerusalem where God dwells with man.
Gog and Magog appear again in Revelation's account of the final battle after the millennium.
The glory of God illuminating the land, paralleling the Lamb as the light of the New Jerusalem.
Waters flowing from the temple giving life, paralleling the river of the water of life from God's throne.
A city with twelve gates named after the tribes, paralleling the New Jerusalem in Revelation.
Echoes Moses' charge to be strong and of good courage, for the Lord will not forsake you.
The command to fear not and be of good courage appears throughout prophetic literature.
Rahab's faith is celebrated in the New Testament as an example of saving faith.
The Jordan crossing parallels the Red Sea crossing, both accomplished by divine power.
The walls of Jericho fell by faith after being compassed seven days.
The sun and moon standing still is referenced as a display of divine power in battle.
Elijah echoes Joshua's challenge to Israel to choose between God and false gods.
Cyrus's decree fulfills Jeremiah's prophecy that after seventy years God would cause the return to Jerusalem.
Cyrus named by Isaiah as the shepherd who would decree the rebuilding of the temple.
The people sing praises echoing the refrain that the mercy of the LORD endures forever.
Parallels the joyful celebration of Passover with the great assembly in Hezekiah's time.
References the wall and restoration of Jerusalem, connecting to Nehemiah's later rebuilding effort.
Nehemiah's opening prayer closely parallels Daniel's intercessory confession, invoking the covenant-keeping God.
Nehemiah echoes Moses's charge to not be afraid because the LORD fights for his people.
The joy of the LORD as strength parallels the psalmist's declaration that the LORD is his strength and shield.
The prayer begins with God as creator of heaven and earth, echoing the opening of Genesis.
Nehemiah references Solomon's fall through foreign wives as a warning against intermarriage.
Paul quotes Hosea's promise that those called 'not my people' shall be called children of the living God.
Peter applies this restoration promise to Gentile believers who were once not a people but are now God's people.
Jesus quotes this verse twice, teaching the Pharisees that God desires mercy rather than sacrifice.
Matthew applies 'Out of Egypt have I called my son' to the return of Jesus from Egypt.
Paul quotes the triumph over death and the grave in his resurrection discourse.
Peter quotes this prophecy at Pentecost, declaring the outpouring of the Spirit as fulfillment.
Paul quotes Joel's promise that whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be delivered.
The harvest and winepress imagery parallels the final judgment in Revelation.
